title: Digital Twins in Critical Infrastructure: Opportunity and Security Challenge
excerpt: Digital twin adoption in utilities, manufacturing, and transport is accelerating. But connecting virtual models to live systems creates attack surfaces that security teams are only beginning to understand.
author: PHEXARA Team
content:
## The promise

Digital twins let operators simulate, predict, and optimise physical systems before committing changes to the real world — reducing downtime, catching failures early, and improving efficiency across utilities, manufacturing, and transport networks.

## The overlooked risk

Every digital twin needs a live data connection back to the physical asset it mirrors. That bidirectional link — often built for speed and convenience rather than security — becomes a new pathway into operational technology (OT) environments that were historically air-gapped.

## What good practice looks like

- Treat the twin-to-asset connection as a security boundary, not an internal pipe
- Apply the same access controls and monitoring to simulation environments as production ones
- Validate that write-back capabilities (twin → physical system) are scoped as narrowly as possible

Digital twins are one of the highest-value applications of connected intelligence in industrial settings — but only if the security model keeps pace with the connectivity.
